Another big risk that these funds can bring to the table is that their underlying holdings may be of very low quality, which enables them to generate the high yields in the first place. For example, if you load up on highly leveraged funds that invest in junk-rated debt (JNK) or, in some cases, mortgage REITs (MORT) or high-yield, high-risk securities, I am sure you have some diversification that does provide a degree of security. Ultimately, if the economy goes down, these securities are likely to suffer tremendously too, which could lead to huge capital losses and painful dividend cuts.

Ultimately, no matter how you slice it, these are all major cons to this approach, and perhaps the worst one of all is that you are loading up on holdings that are either of bad quality or employ some form of financial engineering to generate the distributions. That means that your sequence of return risk is really no better than if you were to simply invest in a low-cost diversified ETF, yet you are paying much higher fees and, in some cases, take on significant leverage risk to do so. At the very least, you are simply holding probably diversified portfolios of securities that, while they may pay out high yields that support the fund's distribution, and they may even be somewhat quality and defensive, they are poor long-term compounders because of their lack of strong underlying growth. Additionally, by paying higher expense ratios for these funds, you are further reducing the total return proposition, and since they are large and diversified, they tend not to implement effective ongoing value investing principles that could be used to accelerate the compounding process.

Why My Value Investing Approach Is Superior

So why is my approach better? First of all, it does not implement leverage. It does not invest much, if at all, in high-cost funds, so it does not have expense ratios eroding gains, and the passive income it generates from investments is fully generated by the holdings that are supported by cash flows, so therefore they are much more doable, durable, and truly separated from the stock market price performance in terms of sequence of returns risk. It also does not use covered calls to generate income, further reducing market volatility and sequence of returns risk.

Perhaps best of all, though, is the fact that by focusing on individual stocks, bonds, and preferreds, and only selectively using ETFs and CEFs for diversification purposes and to take advantage of opportunistic market conditions, I can overlay a value investing and opportunistic capital recycling approach to accelerate the compounding process. Essentially, what this boils down to is by focusing on mid- to high-yielding dividend stocks that have high-quality balance sheets and business models and solid growth potential over time, I can value these securities with a much higher degree of conviction than I would be able to with a broadly diversified fund with scores or even hundreds of underlying holdings or some high-growth, high-valuation, multiple, low-to-no-yield tech stock (XLK).

Therefore, I have a much higher degree of confidence that I am either buying it at an attractive valuation or selling it at around fair value. I can then more effectively implement opportunistic capital recycling to buy stocks on the cheap, sell them when they reach fair value, and rinse and repeat to accelerate the compounding process. This way, I can turn high single-digit to low double-digit annualized total returns into mid- to high-teens annualized total returns and dramatically accelerate dividend growth as well by recycling capital from lower-yielding stocks that have reached fair value to higher-yielding undervalued alternatives.

Investor Takeaway

While the approach of buying a basket of high-yield funds and letting the cash distributions serve as the total return profile with little to no need for growth certainly sounds simple and appealing, as this article points out, it has numerous major flaws with it. Instead, by implementing value investing principles to select individual stocks, bonds, and preferreds while also complementing that with a select few funds, opportunistically, investors can put their portfolio on a much firmer foundation while generating a very attractive high single-digit current yield and significant dividend growth and total returns over time. This is what we have been able to do to outperform the market over time in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Post-FOMC Weakness: U.S. Dollar Breakdown Continues After 25bps Cut
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US Dollar took a hit following yesterday's Fed decision, driven by mechanical flows typical after a rate cut.

Given the rally to new cycle highs over the past two months, current outflows aren't surprising—benefiting not just the majors, but especially exotic currencies.

Prior to the meeting, the Dollar had held relatively strong despite a dovish Fed repricing, ranging near its recent highs supported by elevated yields following Williams' comments.

However, despite the Dot Plot projecting only ~2 cuts for 2026 and Chair Powell suggesting the US is entering the "high end of the neutral rate range," the Dollar is sliding back to mid-October levels.

The cut itself was neither explicitly dovish nor hawkish; communications were solid.

If you missed Powell's speech (great recap right here), he placed extra emphasis on inflation. Consequently, as noted yesterday, future inflation data may carry even more weight than the NFP (which is projected to ease gradually over the next year).

The Fed has been driving blind with limited inflation reports since September due to the government shutdown, making upcoming prints critical for markets.

November NFP is scheduled for December 16, followed by CPI on Dec 18.

Let's dive into the Dollar Index (DXY) charts to spot how much deeper the Dollar can correct given this shift in fundamentals.

Dollar Index (DXY) Multi-timeframe Outlook

Daily Chart

The Dollar is falling harshly after trying to hold its elevated range, nothing too surprising here – Yesterday's move was based from its 200-Day Moving Average acting as resistance and now breaking below its key Pivot Area rendering the DXY bearish short-term.

Looking back even further, the US Dollar is holding a long-term rangebound trajectory since mid-2025 between 97.00 to 100.00 – Makes sense when looking at its first-half harsh fall (-12% since January).

On the bigger picture, cuts have been expected for a long-time in the US so yesterday did not surprise Macro traders too much.

Still, with the Daily RSI going towards the bearish territory and momentum getting strong, the fall should continue at least towards the 98.00 Support area, allowing other majors to appreciate.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Levels to place on your DXY charts:

Resistance Levels

  • 100.00 to 100.50 Main resistance zone
  • 100.376 November highs
  • 99.80 mini-resistance
  • 98.50 to 98.80 Pivot Zone

Support Levels

  • 98.25 Lower bound of 4H Channel
  • 98.00 Key support (+/- 100 pips) Next support
  • 97.40 to 97.80 August Range Support
  • 2025 Lows 96.40 to 96.80 Support

1H Chart

Watch to the reactions as the Dollar reaches the lower bound of the Channel between 98.20 to 98.30 and spot how other assets correlate.

There has been profit-taking flows overnight in Equities so keep an eye on this and flows in other FX currencies throughout the session.

Safe Trades!

data.title: Silver Steadies With Buyers Defending Momentum In Supply-Driven Rally (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Silver pushed deeper into record territory this week, holding above $62 per ounce on Thursday as traders absorbed the Federal Reserve’s latest policy signals and a softer dollar reduced the cost of holding precious metals. Chair Jerome Powell’s remarks leaned more dovish than expected, even as guidance on future rate cuts stayed cautious.

That was enough to trigger another extension in a market already supported by tightening physical conditions and rising investment flows. Physical market behavior has become a defining factor. Borrowing costs and lease rates have risen, signaling genuine delivery stress rather than speculative positioning pressure. That dynamic gives the current rally a firmer foundation than previous cycles, with industrial and investment forces aligning more tightly than at any point this year.

Technical structure remains one of the strongest in commodities

The daily chart shows a steep and orderly uptrend. Silver is trading comfortably above the 20, 50, 100 and 200-day EMAs, with each moving average sloping higher. The 20-day EMA near $56 has served as the primary launchpad for the breakout, and every dip this quarter has found buyers sooner than the one before it.

Silver price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Momentum reflects the same strength. RSI has pushed into the upper-70s, an overbought region that typically signals trend continuation rather than weakness when fundamentals remain aligned. The pattern mirrors the earlier surge seen this year, only now supported by deeper physical constraints and stronger macro inflows.

Short-term action on the 30-minute chart reinforces bullish control. The Supertrend indicator remains positive, and SAR dots are attempting to shift back beneath price, a formation often associated with renewed intraday upside. Pullbacks into the $61.3–$61.4 region have been absorbed quickly, including after the most recent retreat from $62.5. As long as the Supertrend base at $61.3 holds, intraday rhythm favors buyers.

Macro forces and structural demand deepen silver’s rally

The broader narrative has tilted more decisively in silver’s favor. Inflation expectations have stabilized while real rates have stopped rising, and a softer dollar following the Fed’s announcement adds further support. Safe-haven flows have also re-emerged amid lingering geopolitical risks, reinforcing silver’s role within both the defensive and monetary-sensitive asset classes.

Industrial demand remains the most powerful structural force. The energy and data transition continues to reshape global consumption patterns. Solar panel production remains one of the largest and fastest-growing consumers of physical silver, while electric vehicles and server-farm components have expanded their footprint within the metal’s supply chain.

Independent surveys show deficits becoming structural rather than cyclical, with mine supply unable to keep pace as ore grades fall and new projects face multi-year delays.This combination of macro support, supply stress and industrial tightness has pushed silver into a self-reinforcing trend. Markets are now treating the metal not only as a monetary hedge but as a critical resource whose long-term equilibrium price may still be rising.

If momentum holds, silver could test $63.5 in the coming sessions, with $65 emerging as the next psychological marker. Only a break below $60.5 would signal fatigue, though deeper moving averages remain widely supportive.

Outlook as silver builds on its breakout

Silver continues to trade like a market with unfinished upside. Policy shifts have reduced headwinds, physical constraints are deepening, and industrial demand remains robust. Unless liquidity conditions shift abruptly or the dollar stages a sharp reversal, the bias stays pointed higher.

Previously, we discussed how the alignment of industrial demand, tight physical supply and macro inflows created a unique setup for silver to outperform other precious metals. The current expansion above $61 confirms that dynamic, with the breakout supported by both structural and cyclical tailwinds.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

data.title: Real Estate Is Vulnerable Going Into 2026
data.description:
Regular readers of mine will know that I've been cataloging the 2025 real estate market, particularly commercial ("CRE") like it's my obsession or something. They will also likely remember this chart, which captivated my attention back in September when I wrote, "13 Charts That Explain The Current Real Estate Market."

The decline of office construction wasn't sudden, but it was quick. This chart has been burned into my mind, as it perfectly encapsulates the trend I'm seeing; not only are office workers having their roles shifted to AI, but their office demand has shifted to data center demand.

But the whole real estate market is in a weird spot, and I thought it prudent to cover where we are as we head into year's end.

CRE Is Still Really Weak

Commercial real estate has been roiling for some time, and it's looking like the recovery won't be quick, but it's coming. With the Fed lowering interest rates, we should expect to see more recovery, but that is only part of the picture. Demand just isn't what it used to be, and that's very well reflected in pricing; the index price is still below its pre-2020 levels.

CRE is still down from its '22 peaks, and recent growth is showing that it will take a long time in the current economic environment to recover. Of course, a sudden return to zero interest rate policy ("ZIRP"), which is what fueled the surge in 2021-2022 in the first place, could disrupt this. But without that kind of intervention, which I find unlikely, the index is chugging along sluggishly.

Year-to-date, the commercial REIT index has underperformed the broad REIT index fairly dramatically, clocking in a loss of nearly 9% compared to the broad index's 3% gain.

This may spell opportunity for some, but it's unclear to me what the catalyst for recovery will be, especially with the political buzz around turning office buildings into residential complexes in urban centers. This could take some commercial off the market, making the rest more valuable, but it's unclear to me what the effects of this transition will be just yet.

What is clear is that not all CRE is created equal. While I've mostly focused on office buildings, that's just because they're the hardest hit. We've also seen malls take big hits in the last few years and stay at lower levels alongside offices.

What's actually done well are industrial CRE (warehouses, factories, etc.), manufactured homes, and self-storage. These categories have all gained between 40-50% since 2018, although they are all still below their '22 highs as well.

This split isn't likely to correct anytime soon from my view, as the structural and cultural changes that brought on the steep declines in these beat-down sectors like offices and malls are unlikely to reverse. They may pick up in certain rationalities, especially those that are attracting talent and building more jobs (which often require more CRE), like in the Northeast and South. Any recovery we do see is likely to remain regional.

Residential Real Estate Is In a Weird Spot

The story is much more complex for residential real estate, namely because of the market distortions created by the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage -- this is something I detailed in my September article, "The National Housing Emergency," but is easily seen through this chart, which shows US housing prices against western economies where adjustable-rate mortgages ("ARM") and 15-20yr timelines are more common.

Prices are buoyed for residential by the droves of unmotivated sellers. I get it -- if your mortgage is lower than current rates, why move? Even if you break even on the house-to-house move, your monthly payments will skyrocket if you go from a 3% to 6% mortgage.

And mortgage rates are really sticky. Despite Fed cuts, the long end of the curve has risen over the last year, and that has meant mortgage rates are staying elevated too; they tend to track the 10yr UST rate.

While there has been some downward pressure in the last few months, rates still haven't broken below 6%, a level that has seemed to act as resistance in the chart in the past, like in late 2024 when we saw a similar fall.

To deal with this decreased demand (especially from first-time homebuyers), home builders have been offering steeper and steeper incentives all year. Firms like Lennar (LEN) are now offering incentives at levels we haven't seen since 2009. They can't offload their inventories, so they have to make them more attractive to homebuyers.

The incentives are so high across the board, not just at Lennar but also at KB Homes (KBH) and PulteGroup (PHM) too. It's gotten so bad that we're seeing new construction premiums effectively hit zero. New homes are essentially the same cost as older homes currently.

That's not a sign of a healthy housing market.

Residential Buyers Are In Trouble

While there was a surge of new 90-day delinquencies in residential mortgages this year, it seems to have already peaked. The chart below shows the total amount at the top left and then the reasons given by delinquent homeowners in the other charts. Interestingly enough, and I can't easily explain this, the only category still growing into the end of the year is "no contact," with a surge up to 20k mortgages now ghosting their lender.

The folks buying new homes are getting scarcer. The median age of a homebuyer in 2025 is 59, with the median age for first-time homebuyers now at 40.

I was serious when I wrote that the US has built "An Economy Not Built For The Young." Compound this with divergent data on children; we're seeing a record low of young children (under-18s) living at home, while we're moving toward a record high for adult children moving back in with their parents.

None of that makes me very bullish on residential home purchases, especially if mortgage rates stay where they are. I expect that so long as rates stay where they are, the group of unmotivated sellers will continue to hold onto their inventory, which in turn will keep prices up. A slower market for residential is already upon us and will likely continue into next year.

Institutional Buyers Are Coming Back

One positive note for real estate is that institutions, "smart money," are re-entering the space as net buyers in 2025, breaking the net selling trends from 2023-2024. This reversal may be temporary, especially since it is so small, but could also be a signal for more buying to come next year.

To me, it looks like smart money is calling the bottom for real estate and coming back into the space. Maybe they believe the shifts from work-from-home and return-to-office, etc., have finally fully played out, and they're willing to pick from the discount bin.

This could be a catalyst for commercial REITs, especially those that have been beaten down over the last few years. Now, they are able to use whatever cash they have managed to keep or raise through sales in the last few years to deploy into new opportunities. Net buying is a positive for the market, but without the same depth as we saw pre-2020, I am still cautious about relying on this as any kind of surefire signal.

Conclusion

The real estate market is in a tenuous position. Commercial real estate is still largely discounted from its old highs, and commercial buyers are re-entering the space, although the divergence between CRE categories continues with little end in sight for the pain to offices.

Residential homes are likely to retain their prices moving forward, largely motivated by structural factors like the "lock-in effect," which has created droves of unmotivated sellers who stand to lose by moving into a new mortgage due to higher rates. I believe that will be the case for next year, despite the pain we're seeing among residential homeowners as the median age grows and more adult children move back in with them.

The most major takeaway for prospective homeowners is to consider a new construction, as incentives are currently so high that it may be a similar price to an older home. Consider that refinancing into a lower rate in the future is possible, but by that time, the premium on new constructions is likely to move back to more normal levels.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: This Hasn't Happened Since 2018, And Could Redefine The Course Of The Brazilian Elections
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my hold recommendation on ETFs that track the main Brazilian indices. This article is the continuation of my initial coverage thesis published on March 27, 2024, and my last article on the topic was published on July 11, 2025.

In recent days, major events have occurred that could change the course of the Brazilian presidential elections in 2026, and consequently the performance of Brazilian assets. My intention is to address these themes and connect them with my investment thesis.

Brazilian Assets

Without a doubt, the main ETF that tracks Brazilian assets is the iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (EWZ), which has 0.59% expense ratio and $6 billion in AUM. However, there are other options with more attractive expense ratio, such as Franklin FTSE Brazil ETF (FLBR). As we can see below, the performance of these assets has been interesting this year, but is this due to a structural movement towards improving Brazilian macroeconomic conditions? I will address this next.

The EWZ ETF tracks the performance of the MSCI Brazil 25-50 Index. As a reference, the MSCI Brazil 25-50 Index presents a performance of 47.81% year to date, while the EWZ presents a performance of 47.60% according to the Seeking Alpha, so the tracking error is very low.

The ETF has 56 holdings, as we can see below. The most relevant company is Nu Holdings, Ltd. (NU), followed by Petróleo Brasileiro S.A. - Petrobras (PBR) and Vale S.A. (VALE). The discussion of this article is very relevant because Petrobras has the Brazilian Government as its main shareholder, while Vale and other companies such as AXIA Energia SA (AXIA) have the presence of Brazilian Government nominees on their boards of directors. That said, political developments have a great influence on this ETF, especially on Petrobras shares.

Context

On July 11, 2025 I wrote about the 50% tariffs on Brazilian products, and what investors should do with their Brazilian assets at that time. In general, I recommended that investors not make any decisions based on tariffs, but focus on the fundamentals of the investment, and I consider the recommendation to be correct.

Since then, many tariffs have been repealed and the ETF's performance has been interesting, but I credit this to the international scenario, and not to the Brazilian domestic scenario, and I will support this opinion with arguments in this article.

Deep Analysis

Recently, I wrote an article about the change in the Trump administration's behavior towards the Brazilian Government, and I related this to the importance of Brazilian rare earths. The event mentioned was relevant in my opinion. However, we had another major event occur in Brazil, and my intention is to leave investors well-informed to make the best decision.

Recently, former president Jair Bolsonaro was sentenced to 27 years and imprisoned for a coup d'état, and Brazilian justice made him ineligible. Therefore, there were great expectations who he would support in the 2026 presidential elections. Many believed that the former Brazilian president would support the current governor of the state of São Paulo, Tarcísio de Freitas. Tarcísio is a candidate who pleases voters on the left and right, and has a good reputation as Governor of São Paulo, the largest state in Brazil.

Although President Lula makes gaffes several times, it is undeniable that he is extremely competitive if he is to contest his fourth election. In this sense, Bolsonaro's support is extremely important, as he could indeed decide the next election, and his decision took everyone by surprise.

The Candidate Supported By Jair Bolsonaro

Former President Jair Bolsonaro chose to support his eldest son, Senator Flávio Bolsonaro, who apparently will be a candidate for President of the Republic in the next elections. Although Senator Flávio Bolsonaro has a very political profile, the Brazilian financial market did not like this decision and shares fell sharply in recent days due to this choice, to the detriment of Tarcísio Freitas.

This is a bold strategy in my opinion, and I explain why. As I said, former President Bolsonaro is ineligible for public office such as the Presidency of the Republic, therefore, Bolsonaro must hope that his chosen candidate wins the presidential elections.

If the candidate chosen by Bolsonaro does not win the elections, there is a great chance that he (Bolsonaro) will remain in prison for a long time, given that the presidency of the republic is dominated by the opposition (Workers' Party) and the majority of nominees to the Supreme Court are also nominated by current president Lula. But this strategy can also work.

We must remember that when Lula was arrested in 2018 as part of the Car Wash operation, several left-wing candidates tried to persuade him to support a possible candidate in the elections, and Lula did not do so. He chose to protect his political capital, and when he had the opportunity, he returned to the game and won the elections. It seems to me that Bolsonaro is trying exactly the same, not to transfer his political capital to a third party, but to keep it in his family.

Reflection On The Financial Market

The fact is that the stock market did not fall just because of Flávio Bolsonaro's choice, but because of fear of another four years of Lula's presidency. You may be surprised by this statement of mine, and point out that this makes no sense because the EWZ, which tracks the main Brazilian assets, is rising strongly this year. However, I refute this thesis since the Fed's interest rate cut has boosted assets around the world, and this is the main driver in my opinion.

This is why I remain convinced of my recommendation to hold the EWZ ETF. After all, we are facing an improvement driven by international factors. But when we look at Brazil, we see that it is a country with extremely high-interest rates, a GDP that shows low growth compared to other economies, and what's worse, it is a country that has little room for increasing federal revenue. Therefore, I see little evidence that Brazil will present a great performance if current economic policies are maintained for another four years.

Another factor that makes me pessimistic about Brazilian assets is that, in the last 3 years, the average P/E was 10x earnings, and we are already at 11x profits with the recent price spike. Therefore, the risk-return ratio is no longer as attractive, which corroborates my recommendation to hold assets that track the main Brazilian indices.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

After all this construction of ideas, there is no doubt that the main risk to the thesis is the success of this movement by the Bolsonaro family. The most curious thing is that perhaps this has started to occur. I say this because the Brazilian Chamber of Deputies approved the dosimetry bill that reduces the sentence for those convicted of a coup d'état. In the case of Jair Bolsonaro, the sentence would drop from 27 years to just 2 years. Of course, the project still needs to be approved by the Senate.

Therefore, the appointment of Flávio Bolsonaro shows that the Bolsonaro family still has relevant bargaining power over Brazilian politics. We can imagine a scenario where Flávio Bolsonaro makes an alliance with Tarcísio de Freitas, and in this case, there is a real chance that President Lula will be defeated.

This would make Brazilian assets soar, and I'm not saying that based on my political opinions, after all that's beside the point. But the behavior of Brazilian assets after the announcement that Jair Bolsonaro would support his son indicates this.

The Bottom Line

Investment in Brazilian assets has never been known for stability, but rather for instability, and is no different in the 2026 perspectives. The financial market projected a presidential dispute between President Lula and candidate Tarcísio de Freitas, with the support of Bolsonaro.

However, we have reached the end of 2025 and former president Bolsonaro has indicated that he will support his eldest son for the presidential race, Senator Flávio Bolsonaro. This completely changes the political landscape in 2026.

Although this bold bet could be successful, it is a great risk to hand the elections back to President Lula and, consequently, maintain many of the economic measures that, in my opinion as an investment analyst, will not make progress in improving the performance of Brazilian assets in the future.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to hold assets that track the main Brazilian indices. In my opinion, despite the increase this year, the risk-return relationship for long-term investment in these assets is not yet attractive.
